schools.summary: Henry: the private anointing is God's own act, valid despite mean circumstances; the kiss is homage and glad consent.
Gill: common oil from a brittle vial, a smaller measure of the Spirit and a token of a short reign.
Clarke and Wesley: a simple ancient rite; Saul is made captain (nagid) over the Lord's inheritance, not yet crowned king.
Ellicott: the vial dripped oil; the kiss was reverential homage; the oil was the sacred anointing ointment used for founders of dynasties.

debates.question: Was the oil common oil or the sacred anointing oil?

sides.position: common oil (or balsam), a brittle vial signifying a short reign and small measure of the Spirit
the holy anointing ointment of the sanctuary, used for founders of dynasties
homage, allegiance, and glad consent
friendship and affection

schools.summary: Keil, Ellicott, Clarke: the three signs accredit Saul as the Lord's anointed; the tomb and Zelzah are located and the LXX's verbal reading of Zelzah noted.
Gill: Rachel's sepulchre lay some miles from Ramah, near Bethlehem.
Wesley: the sepulchre on the tribal border reads a lecture of mortality to the man who now has a crown in view.

sides.position: an unidentified place-name, perhaps Zela of Benjamin
the LXX takes it as a verb — 'dancing/springing vehemently' or 'in great haste'

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: read 'terebinth (oak) of Tabor'; note but reject Ewald's Tabor=Deborah conjecture; Bethel a pilgrim shrine Samuel frequented.
Clarke and Wesley: the men bear sacrificial provisions to Bethel, sacred since Jacob's vision; Wesley keeps the older 'plain' rendering.
Gill simply marks the next stage of Saul's journey.

debates.question: Is the landmark a 'plain' or a 'terebinth/oak' of Tabor?

sides.position: the terebinth (oak) of Tabor, a specific tree on the road
a 'plain' bearing the name, not at Mount Tabor

schools.summary: Clarke: the salutation is the Eastern greeting of peace, a token of friendship; the loaves are provision for the journey.
Keil: the greeting and two loaves are the second accrediting sign given on the day of Saul's calling.
Gill: strangers salute Saul in the common way, their hearts providentially disposed toward him.

themes: the first gift to Israel's first king

schools.summary: Ellicott: the schools of the prophets were Samuel's great work of higher education; most 'sons of the prophets' were not seers but trained preachers, with music carefully cultivated.
Clarke and Wesley: 'prophesying' here means prayer, praise, and inspired song, not prediction — Clarke argues the word's root is intercession.
Gill: the hill is Geba by its Philistine garrison; Saul is enabled to compose and sing psalms untaught.

sides.position: Geba, a city of Benjamin with a Philistine garrison
Gibeah of God, Saul's own city
prayer, supplication, and praise, not prediction
preaching and inspired singing of psalms by the Spirit's impulse

schools.summary: Keil: more than ecstatic contagion — the Spirit transforms Saul's whole disposition, yet this is not Christian regeneration but a change resembling it.
Wesley: the Spirit 'leaps' upon Saul, endowing him with another spirit — knowledge, courage, wisdom, and magnanimity for his office.
Gill: a spirit of prophecy enabling Saul to compose and sing psalms untaught.

debates.question: Is Saul's change a saving regeneration or an equipping for office?

sides.position: a change resembling regeneration but not saving — a transformed disposition for the task
a sudden endowment of gifts fitting him to reign

themes: gifts for office vs saving grace

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: 'do what thy hand findeth' — act as circumstances require, assured of God's help; the first occasion is the Ammonite war.
Clarke: fear nothing, for the signs prove God's guidance. Wesley cautions: act only as call and opportunity come, not seizing the crown prematurely.
Gill: Saul may follow his own prudence now that his commission is sealed.
Gill: not obeyed at once but a standing rule, looking to the later Gilgal where Saul fails to wait and is reproved.
Ellicott: the warning guards Saul from usurping the priestly sacrifice at the later Gilgal assembly; Keil weighs the conditional grammar.
Wesley: the command was not immediately performed; Clarke refers the seven days to the confirmation after the Ammonite victory.

debates.question: To which Gilgal occasion does the seven-day command point?

sides.position: the later trial where Saul fails to wait and is reproved
Samuel's coming to confirm the kingdom after the Ammonite victory

schools.summary: Henry: the new heart turned a husbandman into a statesman and prince; whom God calls to a service he makes fit for it.
Keil: 'God changed to him another heart' means God changed and re-disposed him; the signs fell out that very day.
Gill: the change came as Saul turned to depart from Samuel.
Keil and Ellicott: this sign is narrated fully because it was public; Ellicott sees the evening Spirit as sequel to the morning's new heart.
Wesley: only this sign is described because it was the most eminent, permanent, and public; Clarke refers to his note that prophesying is praise.
Gill notes simply Saul's arrival at the hill.

Keil and Ellicott: the amazement shows Saul's former life was rough and uncultured, unlike the prophets' disciples; the incident recurs and becomes a proverb, paralleling Paul among the preachers of Christ.
Wesley: Saul was never instructed in or inclined to such things.
Gill: many townsfolk knew Saul personally, which sharpened the wonder.
Keil and Ellicott: 'who is their father?' means prophecy is God's free gift, not inherited by birth, so God can give it to Saul too; they reject the LXX 'his father' reading.
Wesley: God is the prophets' father; inspiration comes from him, not parentage — give God the glory. Clarke notes the LXX adds 'is it not Kish?'
Gill: a fellow-citizen marvels how one of mean education gained such company and gift.

debates.question: Does 'who is their father?' ask about the prophets' God-given gift or about Saul's father Kish?

sides.position: prophecy is a free gift of God, not an inheritance, so God can give it to Saul
the LXX/Vulgate/Syriac read 'his father' — i.e., who is Kish?

schools.summary: Gill: the prophetic gift was transient, given like the seventy elders' to make Saul respectable before his election.
Keil: Saul's silence about the kingdom springs from unambitious humility, not fear of envy.
Wesley: Saul returns to the high place to praise God and seek counsel; Ellicott sees him desiring solitary communion after the ecstasy.

debates.question: Why did Saul say nothing of the kingdom?

sides.position: unambitious humility and modesty
fear of unbelief and envy (Thenius, Josephus) — rejected by Keil

schools.summary: Gill: the uncle is Ner, father of Abner (Targum, Aquila); Josephus makes it Abner, Saul's best-loved kinsman.
Ellicott: the uncle is probably the ambitious Abner, struck by the change in Saul; Keil notes Saul answers only about the asses.
Clarke: the Hebrew word may mean not 'uncle' but some familiar friend.

sides.position: a named kinsman — Ner or the ambitious Abner
perhaps not an uncle at all but a familiar friend

schools.summary: Gill: the uncle's insistent question betrays a rising suspicion about the kingdom, since all Israel was awaiting Samuel's naming of a king.
Ellicott: Saul's silence best explained by Samuel's charge to keep the secret for himself alone, guarding against intrigue; his modesty proves the changed heart.
Wesley: he told it not in obedience to Samuel and from humble modesty.
Gill: Saul answers only about the asses.

debates.question: Why did Saul withhold the matter of the kingdom?

sides.position: obedience to Samuel's charge of secrecy and humble modesty
the Israelite's caution, fear of betraying a secret prematurely

schools.summary: Henry: the assembly is 'unto the Lord'; before producing the king, Samuel reproves Israel for rejecting the government of a prophet for a captain.
Ellicott and Keil: 'unto the Lord' means Urim and Thummim were present; Mizpeh was chosen for the memory of the earlier victory won there by prayer.
Gill: the elders as representatives are summoned to Mizpeh in Benjamin, the priest and perhaps the ark present.
Keil and Ellicott: the recital of the Exodus deliverances is an indictment of Israel's ingratitude, warning them of the way they take even as God grants their king.
Clarke: these words echo the upbraidings when Israel first demanded a king.
Gill: Samuel speaks in the Lord's name with the prophets' formula.
Keil: the people present themselves by tribes before the altar; Benjamin is 'snatched out' by the Lord through the lot, as Achan was taken.
Wesley: the lot proves God chose Saul, for its disposal is of the Lord, and forestalls all dispute; Clarke details the fourfold narrowing.
Gill: in seeking another ruler the people rejected God as their king.
Ellicott: the lots were probably drawn from an urn rather than cast as tablets, judging from the Hebrew word used.
Wesley: the kingdom, promised to Judah in love, is now forced from God in anger and so given first to the obscure tribe of Benjamin.
Gill: the tribes' representatives come near to the place of the lot.

debates.question: Why does the lot fall on Benjamin rather than Judah?

sides.position: the kingdom was promised to Judah in love, but this king is granted in anger, so given to an obscure tribe

schools.summary: Keil: the inquiry for the missing Saul was made through the high priest by Urim and Thummim; Ellicott notes Matri is absent from the genealogies (Ewald: corruption of Bikri).
Clarke: through modesty or fear Saul had secreted himself.
Gill: they inquired again to learn not who but where the chosen man was.
Ellicott: certain of the lot, Saul shrank and hid among the baggage; a second oracle drew him forth, and his stature would answer the coming murmur.
Clarke and Wesley: they inquired by Urim (or Samuel's prayer); Saul hid among the carts from a sense of his own unworthiness.
Gill: the further inquiry sought the hiding-place of the man already named by lot.
Keil: Saul's head-and-shoulders stature won the people's favour; Samuel then declares 'the right of the monarchy,' distinct from the despotic 'right of the king.'
Gill: the people ran eagerly to fetch and proclaim their king.
Gill: the choice by lot is God's, so the people cannot object; Samuel appeals to their eyes, and even the heathen Eupolemus owns Saul made king by God's will.
Wesley: 'none like him' points to his stature; the cry is their acceptance and promise of subjection. Clarke: the Hebrew is 'may the king live,' not 'God save the king.'
Keil: it was Saul's stature that won the people's favour.

debates.question: What does the acclamation literally say?

sides.position: 'May the king live' (yechi hammelech) — no word for 'God' is present
rendered 'God save the king,' a shout for long and prosperous reign

schools.summary: Gill: a repetition of the warning about the king's arbitrary power, written and laid up as a witness against the people.
Wesley: the laws by which the kingdom was to be governed, based on Deuteronomy 17 and laid up before the ark; Clarke allows a compact may also have been written.
Ellicott: the 'Law of the Kingdom' defining the king's rights, duties, and limits; the first literary composition since Moses, and Samuel still dismisses the assembly.

debates.question: What was 'the manner of the kingdom' Samuel wrote?

sides.position: a repetition of the warning about the king's arbitrary power, a witness against the people
the constitutional law limiting the king's power, based on Deuteronomy 17

themes: the record laid up before the Lord

schools.summary: Gill: Saul returns humbly to Gibeah, no palace or court, followed only by a few men.
Ellicott: proud fellow-citizens were his earliest attendants; his prudent, conciliating start marks the wisdom of the early reign.
Clarke: the band was personal friends, not an army, fitting his later retired life; Wesley: they gave him honourable conduct home.

debates.question: Who were the 'band of men' with Saul?

sides.position: secret friends and personal adherents, an escort of honour
all the men fit to bear arms — rejected as inconsistent with Saul's later retired life

themes: grace as the source of loyalty

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil: the despisers were likely proud princes of Judah and Ephraim; withholding the customary present was near rebellion; Saul acted 'as a deaf man' in prudent self-control.
Clarke: the presents were the public support due a king; Wesley: Saul's silence showed humility and mercy, as Christ held his peace in the day of his patience, awaiting recompense.
Gill: the children of Belial were lawless men, perhaps wealthy envious ones from larger tribes.
